findings.description: The PDF is a single-page map figure with all data tables, legends, notes, and the map image integrated into one spatial layout. The HTML converts this into a long scrolling page with the map as a small thumbnail image at the top, followed by all textual content linearized vertically, completely losing the spatial map-based layout.
findings.evidence: PDF shows a large map with data tables (SW-1 through SW-5) positioned at their geographic sampling locations on the map. HTML shows a tiny map thumbnail followed by all data rendered as sequential HTML tables and text blocks.

findings.description: The map image in the HTML is rendered as a very small thumbnail that has lost nearly all legibility. The detailed geographic features, sampling locations, property boundaries, and road labels visible in the PDF are not discernible in the HTML rendering.
findings.evidence: PDF shows a large, detailed map occupying most of the page. HTML shows a small, compressed version of the map at the top that is too small to read.

findings.description: The data tables in the PDF are positioned spatially on the map near their respective sampling locations (SW-1/DUP through SW-5), providing geographic context. In the HTML, they are rendered as separate sequential HTML tables with no spatial relationship to the map, losing the connection between data and location.
findings.evidence: PDF: Tables for SW-1, SW-2, SW-3, SW-4, SW-5 are placed at specific map coordinates. HTML: Same tables appear as a vertical sequence of standalone HTML tables below the map image.

findings.description: The reading order has been altered. In the PDF, the map and its associated data tables form an integrated visual unit where the user reads data in spatial context. The HTML linearizes all content: map image first, then title block information, then legend items, then each data table sequentially, then notes, then references.
findings.evidence: PDF: Integrated map-based layout with simultaneous visual access to all elements. HTML: Linear top-to-bottom sequence starting with map thumbnail, then metadata, then tables SW-1 through SW-5 in sequence, then notes.

findings.description: Document has nested <main> elements and uses both h1 and h2 for the title block, but the hierarchy is reasonable. 'SOUTHSIDE PARK LANDFILL' is rendered as h2 under h1 'SURFACE WATER ASSESSMENT', which is acceptable given the figure layout.
findings.evidence: HTML has h1 'SURFACE WATER ASSESSMENT' and h2 'SOUTHSIDE PARK LANDFILL'. In the PDF, 'SURFACE WATER ASSESSMENT' is the main title and 'SOUTHSIDE PARK LANDFILL' is a subtitle/project name.

findings.description: The 'Risk Assessment' row in each table has an empty second cell, and CR/HI are presented as separate rows rather than as sub-items under 'Risk Assessment'. This is a structural choice that doesn't lose data but differs slightly from the PDF's visual grouping.
findings.evidence: PDF shows 'Risk Assessment' as a header with CR and HI as sub-items. HTML shows them as separate table rows with Risk Assessment having an empty value cell.

findings.description: Nested <main> elements - there are two <main> tags which is a minor HTML structural issue but does not affect content.
